rows.title: San Marino: ECtHR Finds Right to Lawful Tribunal Breached
rows.summary: ECHR judgment Simoncini v. San Marino on right to tribunal established by law in disciplinary proceedings — binding precedent on procedural fairness.
rows.outcome: ECHR judgment Simoncini v. San Marino on right to tribunal established by law in disciplinary proceedings — binding precedent on procedural fairness.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Rules on Police Biometric Data Collection in Criminal Probes (C-371/24, Comdribus)
rows.summary: Case C-371/24: CJEU ruled biometric data collection in criminal investigations must meet 'strictly necessary' test with case-by-case reasons; systematic collection violates EU law. Binding precedent on Directive 2016/680.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU ruled biometric data collection in criminal investigations must meet 'strictly necessary' test with case-by-case reasons; systematic collection violates EU law. Binding precedent on Directive 2016/680.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Rules GDPR Access Requests Can Be Abusive (C-526/24, Brillen Rottler)
rows.summary: Case C-526/24: CJEU ruled excessive GDPR access requests for compensation may be abusive; controllers can refuse and data subjects must prove actual damage. Binding precedent on GDPR Article 12 abuse-of-rights.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU ruled excessive GDPR access requests for compensation may be abusive; controllers can refuse and data subjects must prove actual damage. Binding precedent on GDPR Article 12 abuse-of-rights.

rows.title: European Union: AG Opinion — Member States May Ban Huawei on Security Grounds (C-354/24)
rows.summary: Case C-354/24: AG Ćapeta proposed CJEU finding that Member States may exclude telecom hardware/software (e.g. Huawei) on national-security grounds, subject to proportionality. AG Opinion only — Court judgment pending.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: AG Ćapeta proposed CJEU finding that Member States may exclude telecom hardware/software (e.g. Huawei) on national-security grounds, subject to proportionality. AG Opinion only — Court judgment pending.

rows.title: Ukraine: ECtHR Finds Detention Conditions Rights Violated
rows.summary: ECHR judgment finds Ukraine violated Articles 3 & 13 via inadequate detention conditions; no effective remedy available.
rows.outcome: ECHR judgment finds Ukraine violated Articles 3 & 13 via inadequate detention conditions; no effective remedy available.

rows.title: Albania: ECtHR Rules Article 8 Violated in Magistrate Bar Case
rows.summary: ECHR judgment — Manjani v. Albania — struck down absolute professional ban based on spent juvenile conviction; precedent on rehabilitation and proportionality in employment screening.
rows.outcome: ECHR judgment — Manjani v. Albania — struck down absolute professional ban based on spent juvenile conviction; precedent on rehabilitation and proportionality in employment screening.

rows.title: Bulgaria: ECtHR Rules Undercover-Agent Rules Breach Article 8
rows.summary: ECHR rules Bulgaria's undercover-agent infiltration regulations violate Article 8 (privacy) for lacking safeguards against arbitrariness.
rows.outcome: ECHR rules Bulgaria's undercover-agent infiltration regulations violate Article 8 (privacy) for lacking safeguards against arbitrariness.

rows.title: Hungary: AG Kokott Opinion — Sovereignty Law Breaches EU Rules (C-829/24)
rows.summary: Case C-829/24: AG Kokott proposed CJEU finding that Hungary infringed EU law (GDPR Articles 5, 6, 9, 10 + Charter rights) by adopting Law LXXXVII (Sovereignty Protection Office). AG Opinion only — Court judgment pending.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: AG Kokott proposed CJEU finding that Hungary infringed EU law (GDPR Articles 5, 6, 9, 10 + Charter rights) by adopting Law LXXXVII (Sovereignty Protection Office). AG Opinion only — Court judgment pending.

rows.title: Hungary: ECtHR Rules Integrity-Testing Laws Breach Article 8
rows.summary: ECHR strikes down Hungary's integrity-testing regime for state employees — covert surveillance without adequate judicial safeguards violates Article 8.
rows.outcome: ECHR strikes down Hungary's integrity-testing regime for state employees — covert surveillance without adequate judicial safeguards violates Article 8.

rows.title: Ukraine: ECtHR Rules Covert Surveillance Breached Article 8
rows.summary: ECHR Vykhor v. Ukraine — Article 8 (private life), covert surveillance, interception of communications; binding precedent on state surveillance and right to remedy.
rows.outcome: ECHR Vykhor v. Ukraine — Article 8 (private life), covert surveillance, interception of communications; binding precedent on state surveillance and right to remedy.

rows.title: Italy: ECtHR Rules Bank-Data Access Breaches Article 8 (Ferrieri and Bonassisa)
rows.summary: ECHR ruling: Italian tax authority's warrantless banking-data access violates Article 8 (private life); law lacks safeguards — binding precedent on data-protection compliance.
rows.outcome: ECHR ruling: Italian tax authority's warrantless banking-data access violates Article 8 (private life); law lacks safeguards — binding precedent on data-protection compliance.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Rules on GDPR Info Duties for Body Camera Use (C-422/24, Storstockholms Lokaltrafik)
rows.summary: Case C-422/24: CJEU ruled body-camera ticket-inspection data is direct collection under GDPR; immediate transparency obligations under Articles 13/14 apply; multi-layered disclosure permitted.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU ruled body-camera ticket-inspection data is direct collection under GDPR; immediate transparency obligations under Articles 13/14 apply; multi-layered disclosure permitted.

rows.title: Council of Europe: ECtHR Rules on CJEU Referral Refusal Reasoning Duty
rows.summary: ECHR ruled national courts must give reasons when refusing CJEU preliminary-ruling requests — procedural fairness in EU law application affects controllers' remedies.
rows.outcome: ECHR ruled national courts must give reasons when refusing CJEU preliminary-ruling requests — procedural fairness in EU law application affects controllers' remedies.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Rules Online Marketplaces Are GDPR Controllers (C-492/23, Russmedia Digital)
rows.summary: Case C-492/23: CJEU ruled online marketplace operator is GDPR controller; must identify & verify sensitive-data ads before publication; cannot rely on e-Commerce Directive intermediary safe harbours.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU ruled online marketplace operator is GDPR controller; must identify & verify sensitive-data ads before publication; cannot rely on e-Commerce Directive intermediary safe harbours.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Rules on Police Storage of Biometric Data (C-57/23, Policejní prezidium)
rows.summary: Case C-57/23: CJEU ruled police may collect biometric/genetic data with periodic necessity review; no fixed maximum-storage period required. Binding precedent on Directive 2016/680 storage limits.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU ruled police may collect biometric/genetic data with periodic necessity review; no fixed maximum-storage period required. Binding precedent on Directive 2016/680 storage limits.

rows.title: European Union: General Court Dismisses Amazon DSA Designation Challenge (T-367/23)
rows.summary: Case T-367/23: General Court upheld European Commission designation of Amazon Store as Very Large Online Platform under DSA Regulation 2022/2065. Binding precedent on DSA scope and fundamental-rights balancing.
authorities: General Court of the European Union
rows.outcome: General Court upheld European Commission designation of Amazon Store as Very Large Online Platform under DSA Regulation 2022/2065. Binding precedent on DSA scope and fundamental-rights balancing.

rows.title: Poland: ECtHR Rules Privacy Violated in Medical-Records Disclosure
rows.summary: ECHR ruled Poland violated Article 8 (right to private life) by disclosing applicant's medical records to prosecutor without consent or appeal mechanism.
rows.outcome: ECHR ruled Poland violated Article 8 (right to private life) by disclosing applicant's medical records to prosecutor without consent or appeal mechanism.

rows.title: Armenia: ECtHR Finds Privacy Rights Violated in NSS Coercion Case
rows.summary: ECHR Final judgment — Manukyan v. Armenia: unlawful personal-data collection by security services, coercion via threats, violates Article 8 ECHR, no legal basis shown.
rows.outcome: ECHR Final judgment — Manukyan v. Armenia: unlawful personal-data collection by security services, coercion via threats, violates Article 8 ECHR, no legal basis shown.

rows.title: Greece: ECtHR Finds Fair-Trial Rights Violated in Disciplinary Case (Tsatani)
rows.summary: ECHR judgment (Tsatani v. Greece) on impartiality in disciplinary proceedings — binding precedent on fair trial + data processing; Article 6 ECHR violation found.
rows.outcome: ECHR judgment (Tsatani v. Greece) on impartiality in disciplinary proceedings — binding precedent on fair trial + data processing; Article 6 ECHR violation found.

rows.title: Azerbaijan: ECtHR Rules Against Azerbaijan in Journalist Info-Access Case
rows.summary: ECHR ruled journalist's right to access public-interest information (Articles 10, 13) — binding precedent on data access / government transparency obligations.
rows.outcome: ECHR ruled journalist's right to access public-interest information (Articles 10, 13) — binding precedent on data access / government transparency obligations.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Clarifies Personal Data Scope for Pseudonymised Transfers (C-413/23 P, EDPS v SRB)
rows.summary: Case C-413/23 P: CJEU set aside General Court judgment in C-413/23 P (EDPS v SRB) and referred case back. Binding precedent on scope of personal data in pseudonymised-data transfers under Regulation 2018/1725.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU set aside General Court judgment in C-413/23 P (EDPS v SRB) and referred case back. Binding precedent on scope of personal data in pseudonymised-data transfers under Regulation 2018/1725.

rows.title: European Union: General Court Upholds Zalando's VLOP Designation Under DSA (T-348/23)
rows.summary: General Court upheld Commission designation of Zalando as Very Large Online Platform under DSA. Binding precedent on VLP classification and active-recipient counting methodology.
authorities: General Court of the European Union
rows.outcome: General Court upheld Commission designation of Zalando as Very Large Online Platform under DSA. Binding precedent on VLP classification and active-recipient counting methodology.

rows.title: Türkiye: ECtHR Finds Rights Violated in ByLock Terror Convictions
rows.summary: ECtHR: 239 Turkish applicants' convictions based on ByLock encryption-app use violated Art. 6 & 7 ECHR — precedent on encrypted-comms evidence and mass digital-surveillance cases.
rows.outcome: ECtHR: 239 Turkish applicants' convictions based on ByLock encryption-app use violated Art. 6 & 7 ECHR — precedent on encrypted-comms evidence and mass digital-surveillance cases.

rows.title: Hungary: ECtHR Rules Collective Expulsion Ban Violated (H.Q. and Others)
rows.summary: ECtHR H.Q. v. Hungary: violations of Art. 4 Protocol 4 (collective expulsion), Art. 13 (effective remedy), Art. 3 — Article 46 order to Hungary to reform asylum procedures.
rows.outcome: ECtHR H.Q. v. Hungary: violations of Art. 4 Protocol 4 (collective expulsion), Art. 13 (effective remedy), Art. 3 — Article 46 order to Hungary to reform asylum procedures.

rows.title: Russia: ECtHR Rules Rights of Blocked Election Candidates Violated
rows.summary: ECHR ruled Russia violated Articles 8, 10, 11 — warrantless political-data collection without legal framework, retention rules, or deletion mechanism.
rows.outcome: ECHR ruled Russia violated Articles 8, 10, 11 — warrantless political-data collection without legal framework, retention rules, or deletion mechanism.

rows.title: Russia: ECtHR Finds Free Expression Rights Violated
rows.summary: ECHR ruled freedom-of-expression violations (Article 10) + deprivation of liberty & fair-trial breaches (Articles 5, 6) in Russia administrative-offence cases.
rows.outcome: ECHR ruled freedom-of-expression violations (Article 10) + deprivation of liberty & fair-trial breaches (Articles 5, 6) in Russia administrative-offence cases.

rows.title: Russia: ECtHR Finds Free Expression Rights Breached (Ten Cases)
rows.summary: ECHR judgment finding breach of Article 10 in Russian press-restriction cases; binding precedent on freedom-of-expression limits. Ten applicants awarded EUR 10k each.
rows.outcome: ECHR judgment finding breach of Article 10 in Russian press-restriction cases; binding precedent on freedom-of-expression limits. Ten applicants awarded EUR 10k each.

rows.title: Russia: ECtHR Finds Free Expression Rights Breached
rows.summary: ECHR rules on Article 10 freedom-of-expression violations; awarded damages for extremism prosecutions. Binding precedent on state conduct affecting fundamental rights.
rows.outcome: ECHR rules on Article 10 freedom-of-expression violations; awarded damages for extremism prosecutions. Binding precedent on state conduct affecting fundamental rights.

rows.title: Russia: ECtHR Rules Rights Violated in LGBT Workshop Raid
rows.summary: ECHR judgment on police raid, Articles 3, 5, 11, 14 — degrading treatment, unlawful detention, freedom of assembly violations in LGBT context.
rows.outcome: ECHR judgment on police raid, Articles 3, 5, 11, 14 — degrading treatment, unlawful detention, freedom of assembly violations in LGBT context.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Rules GDPR Bars Surgery Proof for Gender Data Rectification (C-247/23, Deldits)
rows.summary: Case C-247/23: CJEU ruled GDPR right to rectification of gender identity data; Member States cannot condition rectification on gender-reassignment surgery. Binding precedent on GDPR Article 16 rectification rights.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU ruled GDPR right to rectification of gender identity data; Member States cannot condition rectification on gender-reassignment surgery. Binding precedent on GDPR Article 16 rectification rights.

rows.title: Portugal: ECtHR Finds Detention Conditions Rights Violated
rows.summary: ECHR ruled inadequate detention conditions violated Article 3 (inhuman/degrading treatment) + lack of effective remedy (Article 13); awarded damages.
rows.outcome: ECHR ruled inadequate detention conditions violated Article 3 (inhuman/degrading treatment) + lack of effective remedy (Article 13); awarded damages.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Fines Germany, Luxembourg, Czechia, Estonia & Hungary for Whistleblower Directive Failure (C-147/23, C-149/23, C-150/23, C-152/23, C-154/23, C-155/23)
rows.summary: CJEU under Article 258/260 TFEU ordered 5 Member States to pay penalties for failure to transpose Whistleblowers Directive 2019/1937 by 17 Dec 2021 deadline. Lump-sum + daily-penalty coefficient methodology.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU under Article 258/260 TFEU ordered 5 Member States to pay penalties for failure to transpose Whistleblowers Directive 2019/1937 by 17 Dec 2021 deadline. Lump-sum + daily-penalty coefficient methodology.

rows.title: Bulgaria: ECtHR Rules Journalists' Access to Acquittal Reasons Breached
rows.summary: ECHR Girginova v. Bulgaria — journalist freedom-of-information + Article 10 rights breach; precedent on public-interest disclosure + classified-information balance.
rows.outcome: ECHR Girginova v. Bulgaria — journalist freedom-of-information + Article 10 rights breach; precedent on public-interest disclosure + classified-information balance.

rows.title: European Union: CJEU Rules on GDPR Explanation Rights for Automated Credit Scores (C-203/22, Dun & Bradstreet Austria)
rows.summary: Case C-203/22: CJEU ruled controllers must explain automated credit-decision logic to data subjects under GDPR Article 15(1)(h); trade-secret claims do not shield explanation. Binding precedent on automated decision-making transparency.
authorities: Court of Justice of the EU
rows.outcome: CJEU ruled controllers must explain automated credit-decision logic to data subjects under GDPR Article 15(1)(h); trade-secret claims do not shield explanation. Binding precedent on automated decision-making transparency.

rows.title: Ukraine: ECtHR Rules Secret Surveillance Violated Rights
rows.summary: ECHR ruling: Ukraine's secret surveillance/phone-tapping lacked lawful safeguards and lawyer-client privilege protection (Article 8 violation).
rows.outcome: ECHR ruling: Ukraine's secret surveillance/phone-tapping lacked lawful safeguards and lawyer-client privilege protection (Article 8 violation).

rows.title: Russia: ECtHR Rules Press Freedom Violated in Ukraine War Reporting (Novaya Gazeta)
rows.summary: ECHR Chamber ruling: 161 media applicants; violations of Articles 10, 5, 8, 34 — prosecution, shutdown, detention for critical coverage of military action.
rows.outcome: ECHR Chamber ruling: 161 media applicants; violations of Articles 10, 5, 8, 34 — prosecution, shutdown, detention for critical coverage of military action.

rows.title: Russia: ECtHR Finds Free Expression Violated in Religious-Offence Cases
rows.summary: ECHR Samodurov v. Russia: violation of Article 10 (freedom of expression) — binding precedent on state interference with expression; landmark on religious-sensibility balancing.
rows.outcome: ECHR Samodurov v. Russia: violation of Article 10 (freedom of expression) — binding precedent on state interference with expression; landmark on religious-sensibility balancing.

rows.title: Russia: ECtHR Finds Russia Failed LGBTI Applicants in Data-Leak Cases
rows.summary: ECHR Third Section rules domestic authorities failed positive obligation to protect applicants' personal data and privacy against non-consensual social-media dissemination.
rows.outcome: ECHR Third Section rules domestic authorities failed positive obligation to protect applicants' personal data and privacy against non-consensual social-media dissemination.

rows.title: Armenia: ECtHR Finds LGBT Activists' Rights Breached in Hate-Speech Case
rows.summary: ECtHR finds Armenia violated Article 8 (private life) and 14 (discrimination) — state failed positive obligation to protect LGBT activists against online hate speech and incitement.
rows.outcome: ECtHR finds Armenia violated Article 8 (private life) and 14 (discrimination) — state failed positive obligation to protect LGBT activists against online hate speech and incitement.

rows.title: ECOWAS Court orders Nigeria to amend Cybercrimes Act § 24 (SERAP v. Federal Republic of Nigeria)
rows.summary: In March 2024 the ECOWAS Court of Justice issued a pivotal ruling in a case initiated by the Socio-Economic Rights and Accountability Project (SERAP). Court determined § 24 of the Cybercrimes Act 2015 contravenes Art. 9 of the African Charter and the ICCPR. The ruling drove the Cybercrimes Amendment Act 2024 (signed 28 February 2024). Decision date approximate; pending exact-day verification.
rows.outcome: § 24 of Cybercrimes Act 2015 contravenes Art. 9 of the African Charter on Human and Peoples' Rights and the ICCPR; Nigeria ordered to amend.

rows.title: Nigeria Okedara v. Attorney General of the Federation (Cybercrimes Act § 24 challenge)
rows.summary: Solomon Okedara v. Attorney General of the Federation (CA/L/174/18). Solomon Okedara challenged the validity of § 24(1) of the Cybercrimes Act 2015 — the offence of sending grossly offensive / menacing messages via a computer system. Court of Appeal, Lagos Division, ruled the language was not ambiguous and rejected the appeal. Decision date approximate; pending exact-day verification.
rows.outcome: Court of Appeal Lagos rejected free-expression challenge; § 24(1) of Cybercrimes Act 2015 upheld.

rows.title: India: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India — Right to Privacy is a Fundamental Right
rows.summary: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India, (2017) 10 SCC 1 — Writ Petition (Civil) No. 494 of 2012, decided 24 August 2017 by a 9-judge bench of the Supreme Court of India. The reference arose from the Aadhaar challenge but the bench was constituted specifically to determine, as a foundational question, whether privacy is a fundamental right. All nine judges (Chief Justice J.S. Khehar plus eight) authored or concurred in opinions affirming privacy as fundamental. The judgment is the foundational precedent for India's entire data-protection regime: it underpins the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023, the DPDP Rules 2025, the IT Act 2000 §43A line of cases, and the November 2025 amendment to RTI Act §8(1)(j) (which the DPDP Rules expressly note is "consistent with the Supreme Court's affirmation of privacy as a fundamental right"). Bench: Chief Justice Jagdish Singh Khehar, Justices J. Chelameswar, S.A. Bobde, R.K. Agrawal, Rohinton Fali Nariman, Abhay Manohar Sapre, Dr. D.Y. Chandrachud, Sanjay Kishan Kaul and S. Abdul Nazeer.
rows.outcome: Unanimous 9-judge constitutional bench held that the right to privacy is a fundamental right protected under Article 21 (right to life and personal liberty) and Part III of the Constitution. Overruled M.P. Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962) to the extent inconsistent. Privacy attaches to the person (not place) and includes informational privacy. Any state intrusion must satisfy a three-fold test: (i) legality (existence of a law); (ii) need (legitimate state aim); (iii) proportionality.
